items.q: What were the Wars of the Roses really about?
items.correct: Dynastic conflict between rival branches of the Plantagenets (Lancaster and York) over the throne, intensified by weak kingship under Henry VI and noble factionalism.
items.misconception: The whole country was at war for thirty continuous years.
items.random_wrong: They were a religious war between Catholics and Protestants.
items.why: Fighting was sporadic and most of England saw no battles; ordinary life carried on. Students imagine total civil war; in reality, it was an aristocratic dispute fought in short campaigns.

items.q: How did Henry VII secure the Tudor dynasty after 1485?
items.correct: Through marriage to Elizabeth of York, ruthless control of the nobility (bonds and recognisances), financial discipline, and crushing Yorkist pretenders like Simnel and Warbeck.
items.misconception: Henry VII was universally popular because he ended the Wars of the Roses.
items.random_wrong: Henry VII was Richard III's nephew and inherited the throne legally.
items.why: Henry's claim was weak; he ruled by control, not consent. Students assume the wars 'ended' at Bosworth, missing how shaky early Tudor rule was.

items.q: Why did Hitler order the Night of the Long Knives in 1934?
items.correct: To eliminate Ernst Röhm and the SA leadership, reassure the army of his authority, and remove rivals — securing the army's loyalty when Hindenburg died weeks later.
items.misconception: Hitler purged the SA because they were Communists.
items.random_wrong: It was a battle in which the SA tried to overthrow Hitler.
items.why: The SA were Nazis, not Communists. Students miss that this was internal consolidation: Hitler traded the SA for army backing, completing the move to total power.

items.q: Which 1533 Act prevented appeals to Rome?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Act of Appeals. “Act of Supremacy” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Who controlled the Council Learned under Henry VII?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Empson and Dudley. “Wolsey and More” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: In what year did Elizabeth I die?
items.why: The real figure is 1603 AD. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
